75995.subject: How To Build Career Security Amid Economic Uncertainty
75995.body:
Forbes Edge

Автор: Forbes


Автор: Forbes

    
    
    Прочитайте цю статтю на LinkedIn, щоб приєднатися до обговорення
Прочитати на LinkedIn
https://www.linkedin.com/comm/pulse/how-build-career-security-amid-economic-uncertainty-forbes-magazine-rshvc?lipi=urn%3Ali%3Apage%3Aemail_email_series_follow_newsletter_02%3B9dP8%2BVcOSQeo364xfDiqZw%3D%3D&midToken=AQFTjFOONAfT2w&midSig=34hsIejJLZKsk1&trk=eml-email_series_follow_newsletter_02-null-0-read_more_banner_cta_&trkEmail=eml-email_series_follow_newsletter_02-null-0-read_more_banner_cta_-null-fz96fz~mrm5ipiy~4n-null-null&eid=fz96fz-mrm5ipiy-4n
  
In a challenging job market that’s increasingly shaped by AI’s impact, it’s become apparent that more workers are saying goodbye to the goal of rapid career growth and are instead embracing the idea of greater career stability.  According to a 2025 survey of more than 1,500 workers from Monster.com, 57% of employees said they plan to stay at their current company through 2026. This can largely be attributed to worker fears tied to economic uncertainty, with 40% of workers saying they expect the job market to get worse this year and 52% saying they expect nationwide layoffs to increase. Additionally, when looking at Gen Z and millennials, who now make up more than half of the U.S. labor force, a report from Deloitte found that a growing number of younger workers are making career choices that prioritize long-term stability over short-term advancement. Forbes senior contributor Tracy Brower links these statistics and economic concerns to the growing need for workers to understand the pragmatic ways in which they can show and prove their value at work. While there is no surefire way to future proof your career from uncertainty, Brower outlines in her recent piece a few things workers can do now if they want to remain relevant, dependable and successful in their field.  Read here to learn why she says staying curious, connected, competent, compelling and creative can increase your chances of career security.  — Courtney Connley-Hampton, Careers Staff Writer WORK SMARTER  Practical insights and advice from Forbes staff and contributors to help you succeed in your job, accelerate your career and lead smarter. Summer is the smartest time to prepare for performance reviews at the end of the year, says leadership expert Esther Choy. Use this simple storytelling tool to spotlight your valuable work. Some workers regret taking a new job despite the initial relief it brings. These job offer red flags can reveal warning signs you may have missed during the interview process, according to executive coach Caroline Castrillon. Public speaking is the ultimate career skill in the age of artificial intelligence, according to personal brand expert William Arruda. See how public speaking will strengthen your career. A job at your dream company? It may take more than networking to land one, says Caroline Ceniza-Levine. Here are three different ways to raise your

75993.subject: CPI Sparks Crypto Rally
75993.body:
View image: (https://media.beehiiv.com/cdn-cgi/image/fit=scale-down,format=auto,onerror=redirect,quality=80/uploads/asset/file/eaa70932-7ce4-467e-b338-a662ed575d6a/0xR_NL_Banner_Heading_Wordmark.jpg?t=1776775276)
Caption: 

Hi all, happy Wednesday! Today, we break down how the softest CPI print of the year completely reversed Monday's geopolitical selloff, sparking a broad risk-on rally led by a massive surge in Privacy. 

Plus, we dive into the data behind Hyperliquid to explain why its shrinking revenues and market share don't tell the whole story.

Let’s get into it.

———————————————————————————

View image: (https://media.beehiiv.com/cdn-cgi/image/fit=scale-down,format=auto,onerror=redirect,quality=80/uploads/asset/file/42e942ec-f31d-4957-8722-a3ac7b121e1e/0xR_NL_Market_Update.png?t=1767631151) [Market Update]
Caption: 

The softest consumer price index (CPI) print of 2026 flipped Monday's geopolitical selloff into a broad rally: BTC +4.3%, everything green except miners. Privacy stole the session at +10.3%, with ZEC ripping into its July 28 Ironwood upgrade. We break down the move below.

View image: (https://media.beehiiv.com/cdn-cgi/image/fit=scale-down,format=auto,onerror=redirect,quality=80/uploads/asset/file/06f51abc-f40e-4d50-8448-8d90849f01ac/image.png?t=1784120318)
Caption: 

Across the majors the week is one round trip. Everything bled into Monday's Hormuz panic, BTC briefly 3% underwater, before Tuesday's CPI reversed the move in a session. BTC leads the benchmarks at +2.1% on the week, ahead of NASDAQ (+1.2%), the S&P 500 (+0.5%), and gold (-1.5%), which gave back its geopolitical bid as inflation cooled. Weekly leadership from an asset that has traded heavy for months reads as either an oversold snapback or early regime change; Fear & Greed at 29 says the market has not decided.

View image: (https://media.beehiiv.com/cdn-cgi/image/fit=scale-down,format=auto,onerror=redirect,quality=80/uploads/asset/file/6546b287-098c-412c-adce-e032b87508c1/image.png?t=1784120318)
Caption: 

Tuesday saw broad green with Privacy the clear standout at +10.3%; most sectors clustered in the 2% to 7% range and miners were the lone red at -1.0%. The bid was June CPI: headline fell 0.4% on the month, the largest drop since April 2020, with annual inflation at 3.5% against 3.8% expected, erasing July 29 Fed hike risk and liquidating $68M of shorts within hours.

View image: (https://media.beehiiv.com/cdn-cgi/image/fit=scale-down,format=auto,onerror=redirect,quality=80/uploads/asset/file/aee44133-9fb1-40ed-9006-986f3e801222/image.png?t=1784120318)
Caption: 

Inside the Privacy index, ZEC (48.3% of the index) did the heavy lifting at +14.3% on the day and +15.7% on the week; DCR quietly leads weekly at +24.4% without a clear catalyst, and XMR (41.1% weight) lagged at +3.1%.

ZEC's bid is upgrade-driven. Ironwood activates July 28 at block 3,428,143, and developers say they are close to mathematically proving the next-generati

75985.subject: The world’s most liveable cities in 2026
75985.body:
The Economist’s week ahead

Автор: The Economist


Автор: The Economist

    
    
    Прочитайте цю статтю на LinkedIn, щоб приєднатися до обговорення
Прочитати на LinkedIn
https://www.linkedin.com/comm/pulse/worlds-most-liveable-cities-2026-the-economist-ptmke?lipi=urn%3Ali%3Apage%3Aemail_email_series_follow_newsletter_02%3BpS5DkCLNRGm8scE0OfWiIQ%3D%3D&midToken=AQFTjFOONAfT2w&midSig=28cq89BSBSKsk1&trk=eml-email_series_follow_newsletter_02-null-0-read_more_banner_cta_&trkEmail=eml-email_series_follow_newsletter_02-null-0-read_more_banner_cta_-null-fz96fz~mrm0wxjq~pz-null-null&eid=fz96fz-mrm0wxjq-pz
  
Sign up for free here to receive this newsletter in your inbox every Saturday. You’ll also get our weekly newsletter with an exclusive introduction from our editor-in-chief. A year ago in this newsletter I wrote, “It will be a relief if the [liveable-cities] index in 2026 shows that the world remains as liveable as it is now.” In a way I got my wish: the latest global average liveability score held steady at 76.1 out of 100, the same as in 2025. But drill down into the regions and it looks less stable. Cities across the Middle East, from Doha to Dubai, dropped down the ranking after they were caught up in the Iran war. Muscat, Oman’s capital, performed the worst, plummeting 14 places. And war-torn Tehran joined war-torn Damascus in the bottom ten. Health-care improvements in every Chinese city in the index helped balance out these plunging scores. As much as I’d love to predict an improvement in liveability over the coming year, given the volatility in the Middle East I’d settle for an uneventful year of stolidity. Alex Selby-Boothroyd, Head of data journalism Editor’s picks The world’s most, and least, liveable cities in 2026 See how 173 cities compare in EIU’s annual ranking Where expat escapees from Dubai end up Will they ever return? Global democracy is in better shape than you think EIU’s annual index suggests an end to the democracy recession Africa’s new middle class is putting down roots in the suburbs For a glimpse of the continent’s future, visit the edges of its burgeoning cities Why Germany’s cities are going broke Bad news for residents, good news for the far right’s electoral chances Go beyond the headlines Get unlimited digital access to The Economist by subscribing today.
Приєднатися до обговорення
